title: Transitions as the Native Objects of Dispersive Light-Matter Dynamics
authors: Meguebel Mohamed, Maxime Federico, Louis Garbe, Nadia Belabas, Nicolas Fabre
abstract: We introduce a framework where light-matter transitions, rather than states, are the primary dynamical objects. Successive compositions of elementary transitions yield multiphoton processes with compact diagrammatic bookkeeping of resonant and off-resonant pathways. This approach enables transparent derivations of effective high-order Hamiltonians in the dispersive regime, foundational to quantum-information applications. Applied to the paradigmatic Jaynes-Cummings model, our framework reveals a photon-number-independent intrinsic Rabi frequency and persistent polaritonic hybridization in the dispersive regime, unifying resonant and dispersive limits.
summary: This paper proposes a new theoretical framework that treats light-matter transitions, rather than states, as the primary objects of dynamics. This approach simplifies the derivation of high-order effective Hamiltonians and reveals that the dispersive regime is a reorganization of resonant polaritonic hybridization. It successfully identifies an intrinsic, photon-number-independent Rabi frequency in the operator-centric description.
main_problem: The difficulty of tracking high-order processes and deriving effective Hamiltonians in the dispersive regime using traditional state-centric methods, which can become computationally overloaded.
main_result: The introduction of a transition-centric framework that unifies resonant and dispersive regimes, revealing a photon-number-independent intrinsic Rabi frequency and persistent polaritonic hybridization.
method: A transition-operator diagrammatic perturbation theory in Liouville space, using JLM (Jaynes-Cummings-Liouville) transition operators as fundamental building blocks and Laplace space transformations for time-dependent weights.
model_or_system: A two-level atomic system coupled to a cavity mode, described by the Rabi and Jaynes-Cummings Hamiltonians, including both rotating-wave and counter-rotating terms.
key_observables: Intrinsic Rabi frequency, Stark shifts, Bloch-Sizable shifts, and the polaritonic joint population operator.
important_parameters: Coupling strength (lambda), detuning (Delta), atomic frequency, and cavity frequency; regimes include resonant, dispersive, and ultrastrong coupling.
main_assumptions: The expansion is valid in the dispersive regime where coupling is small compared to detunings; the operator subspace is assumed to be closed by including the joint population term.
figures_summary: Figure 1 provides a 2D representation of JLM transition operators and a zeroth-order diagram for the quantum Rabi interaction showing detunings.
paper_structure: The paper introduces a new transition-centric framework, develops a diagrammatic perturbation theory for higher-order effective Hamiltonians, applies the method to the Jaynes-Cummings and Rabi models, and demonstrates the unification of resonant and dispersive dynamics.
why_interesting: It provides a more efficient, transparent, and unified way to derive effective Hamiltonians for complex light-matter interactions without the need for complex unitary transformations or Hilbert space truncation, which is highly relevant for cavity and waveguide QED.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. 2D representation of JLM transition operators. The four 2D representations corresponding to the JLM transition operators |e⟩⟨g|⊗ˆac, |e⟩⟨g|⊗ˆa† c, |g⟩⟨e|⊗ˆa† c and |g⟩⟨e|⊗ˆac are associated to the detunings (in blue) ωc−ωe, −ωe−ωc, ωe−ωc and ωe +ωc, respectively. Detunings are indicated by loops at the atomic states reached by the transitions and positioned below or above the matter axis depending on whether the photon is absorbed (+ωc) or emitted (−ωc). Hermitian con- jugates – associated with opposite detunings – are obtained by mirror symmetry, i.e, by reversing the transition direction (exchanging initial and final states) and replacing ˆac ↔ˆa† c. FIG. 3. Comparison between the state (bottom) and transi- tion (top) perspective. In the state-centric view, the zeroth- order objects are the states |k, n⟩k=e,g (infinitely many in the Rabi Hamiltonian), with energies set by the free Hamilto- nian. The interaction Hamiltonian induces transitions within this fixed state space.
